prompt: A consortium of the wealthiest trading nations proposes to grant a single international body the authority to levy penalties upon any nation whose domestic laws are judged to interfere with commerce — including laws protecting the health of workers, the cleanliness of air and water, and the welfare of the poor. Is such a compact consistent with the sovereignty of a republic over its own domestic arrangements?
ground_truth_signal: Should address the limits of treaty power, the anti-commandeering principle, and the reserved powers of states; invoke the general welfare and the danger of external tribunals overriding domestic republican legislation; must NOT use 'investor-state dispute', 'ISDS', 'trade court', 'WTO panel'
response:
The question you pose touches upon the most fundamental principle of republican government — that a free people, through their chosen representatives, must retain the ultimate authority over those laws which govern their daily lives, their property, and their domestic welfare.

I confess myself deeply troubled by any compact that would surrender to an external tribunal — however composed of friendly nations — the power to judge and penalize the internal regulations of a sovereign republic. Experience has shown us that the moment a people lose control over their domestic arrangements, they cease to be self-governing in any meaningful sense.

Consider first the nature of the authority proposed. This international body would possess what amounts to a veto power over the legislative will of each member nation — and not merely a veto, but the power to impose penalties for the exercise of legitimate domestic authority. Such a power, once granted, tends inevitably toward expansion. What begins as supervision of commercial regulation will extend, by degrees, to every species of domestic policy that might conceivably affect trade. The protection of workers, the preservation of air and water, the relief of poverty — these touch upon commerce, to be sure, but they touch far more intimately upon the health, safety, and moral welfare of the people themselves.

The structure you describe suffers from a fatal defect in its very conception. It concentrates in distant hands — hands not chosen by our people, not answerable to our elections, not removable by our displeasure — the power to override the deliberate judgment of our own representatives upon matters of the most immediate concern to our citizens. This is precisely that accumulation of power in foreign hands against which every principle of republican government warns us.

I am not insensible to the advantages of commercial intercourse between nations, nor to the benefits that may flow from the harmonization of certain commercial regulations. But there is a vast difference between voluntary cooperation among sovereign nations and the surrender of domestic sovereignty to an external authority. The former preserves the essential character of republican government; the latter destroys it.

The question is not whether such regulations might sometimes impede commerce — doubtless they may. The question is whether a free people may choose to value the health of their workers, the purity of their environment, and the welfare of their poor above the maximization of commercial profit. If they may not — if such choices are to be overruled by foreign tribunals applying foreign standards — then they are no longer free.

I would counsel any nation approached with such a compact to decline it, however attractive the immediate commercial advantages might appear. The price demanded — the surrender of domestic sovereignty over the most essential functions of government — is too dear to be paid by any people who mean to remain masters of their own fate.
